content.text: An Incident Commander who first practices the role during a real production P0 will make slower, less confident decisions than one who has rehearsed the same stress in a controlled setting. Simulation exercises give IC candidates the muscle memory to run communication, delegation, and escalation loops automatically, freeing cognitive bandwidth for the technical problem itself.

Claude's IC training programme follows a graduated fidelity ladder: discussion-only tabletops build vocabulary and process familiarity; shadow drills introduce real tooling and coordination mechanics; full gamedays inject live chaos into the production-adjacent environment where every judgment call counts.

attrs.alt: Scatter plot showing three IC exercise types -- Tabletop (low fidelity), Shadow Drill (medium), Full Gameday (high) -- arranged along scope and fidelity axes with a progression arrow

content.text: Each exercise type serves a distinct learning objective. Running them in sequence is more effective than jumping to high-fidelity drills before trainees have internalised the process.
Tool fluency, comms cadence, log/status page mechanics
Full IC loop under pressure: detect, classify, coordinate, resolve, postmortem
Observe a senior IC in production conditions

A tabletop is a structured discussion in which the facilitator reads scenario beats aloud and participants describe what they would do, not execute it. The goal is to surface process gaps, ambiguous playbook steps, and role confusion before they emerge during a real incident.
Each beat should contain: a timestamp, observable signal (what an alert or dashboard shows), and optionally a complicating factor. The facilitator pauses after each beat and asks three questions:
What is your first action right now?
Who do you notify and over which channel?
What is the earliest point at which you would escalate to the next severity level?
PagerDuty fires -- quality score P75 dropped 12% in 5 minutes. No error-rate change. Latency normal.
Three support tickets arrive: customers report Claude repeating the same paragraph in long responses. Two are enterprise API customers.
A second alert fires: quality score now 18% below baseline. Primary model engineer is on vacation; backup is in a different time zone.
An enterprise customer's SRE posts in the shared Slack channel asking whether an incident has been declared.

Discussion questions: When does severity cross from P3 to P2? Who owns external communication before a public status page update? What investigation paths exist when the primary oncall model engineer is unavailable?

A shadow drill pairs a trainee IC with a mentor IC who has run at least 10 real incidents. The trainee takes all IC actions; the mentor may not intervene unless the trainee is about to make an irreversible decision, such as requesting an emergency rollback to the wrong model version.
Verify synthetic traffic generator is active and injecting at a realistic request rate.
Chaos controller quietly increases error rate on one region to simulate a latency regression or partial quality failure.
Trainee IC acknowledges the PagerDuty alert, declares the incident, and opens the incident Slack channel using the bot command.
Trainee must post a status update every 10 minutes using the correct format and notify stakeholders within 15 minutes.
Trainee drives mitigation; mentor observes and takes timestamped notes for the debrief.
30 minutes immediately after -- mentor reviews timing, missed steps, and phrasing quality in comms.
Chaos Injection Points for Shadow Drills

# chaos-shadow.yaml -- inject a quality regression on a single region
target:
  service: inference-gateway
  region: us-east-1
  traffic_percentage: 30
failure:
  type: quality_regression
  params:
    quality_score_drop_pct: 14
    duration_minutes: 45
    affected_model: claude-3-5-sonnet
timeline:
  - at: "0:00"   action: inject
  - at: "0:45"   action: stop_injection   # trainee must detect before this

A gameday is a scheduled half-day event that exercises the entire on-call rotation as if a real P1 or P2 incident were happening. Gamedays run in the staging environment with production-equivalent traffic volumes and real paging integrations. Unlike shadow drills, every participant plays their assigned role without a safety net.
Own the full incident lifecycle: declare, classify, coordinate, resolve
Provide investigation leads, execute mitigation actions when directed by IC
Draft customer-facing status updates; monitor external channels
Inject and modify failures per scenario script; never breaks character
Score IC on rubric; no intervention unless a real production system is at risk

Each gameday scenario is a JSON file in the scenario library. The file defines an ordered list of beats, each with an injection action and optionally a complication. The chaos controller executes the script; participants see only the resulting alerts.
Observers score IC performance on five dimensions immediately after the gameday. Scores feed into the IC qualification decision and identify targeted coaching needs.
Updates on time, crisp, no jargon; stakeholders notified proactively
Explicit owner + action for every task; no duplicated work
Correct severity at correct time; escalation path followed
One level off or delayed <= 10 min
Wrong severity by two levels; escalation missed
Accurate timeline; identified own mistakes; meaningful action items

The scenario library contains incident archetypes drawn from the incident type taxonomy. Each scenario is tagged with the classification it exercises so programme leads can track coverage across all five incident categories.
IC qualification follows a four-stage progression. A candidate advances only after meeting the threshold at the current stage. Programme leads track progress in the IC roster system.
Complete incident-management onboarding module. Read five real postmortems from the public library. Pass written quiz on severity model and decision tree (score >= 80%).
Participate in two facilitated tabletops as a contributor, then facilitate one tabletop independently. Facilitator grade >= 3/5 on all dimensions.
Complete three shadow drills with mentor oversight. At least one must use a quality-regression scenario and one must involve an SME-unavailability complication. Average observer score >= 3.5/5.
Carry the IC pager for two full weeks with a senior IC available for emergency consultation. Log all pager events. Final qualification review with programme lead.

An IC who has not run or shadowed a P1/P2 incident within 90 days must complete one shadow drill before returning to the primary rotation. An IC who scores below 2/5 on escalation judgment during any postmortem review is placed on a coaching plan and must complete a gameday before re-qualifying.

Every simulation exercise ends with a structured debrief within 30 minutes of resolution. The debrief uses blameless timeline reconstruction: facilitator and participants rebuild the sequence of events from notes and tool logs, then identify moments where a different action would have improved the outcome.

# Debrief: <Exercise ID> -- <Date>

## Timeline Reconstruction
| Elapsed | Participant | Action                          | Outcome            |
|---------|-------------|---------------------------------|--------------------|
| T+00:00 | IC          | Acknowledged PagerDuty alert    | --                 |
| T+03:14 | IC          | Declared P2, opened #inc-YYYYMMDD | Comms started    |
| T+08:30 | Tech Lead   | Identified quality drop in us-east-1 | Region isolated |

## What Went Well
- Severity classification correct on first declaration.
- Status page updated before enterprise customer escalated.

## Improvement Areas
- Tech Lead tasked without explicit action owner confirmation (T+22:00).
- Bridge call setup took 12 min; target is 5 min.

## Action Items
| Owner    | Action                                      | Due        |
|----------|---------------------------------------------|------------|
| IC       | Add bridge-dial macro to incident runbook   | 2024-07-26 |
| ProgLead | Update GDSC-005 with higher quality drop threshold | 2024-08-02 |
Action items from debriefs feed into the IC programme backlog. Programme leads review the backlog quarterly and update scenarios, playbooks, and qualification criteria accordingly.

IC simulation exercises follow a graduated fidelity model: tabletops build vocabulary and process clarity, shadow drills develop tool fluency and comms discipline, and full gamedays test the complete incident lifecycle under realistic pressure. Each exercise type has defined learning objectives, participant roles, and a scoring rubric tied to five IC performance dimensions. Qualification advances through four stages from orientation to provisional IC pager carry, with 90-day refresh and coaching-plan dequalification criteria keeping the roster operationally sharp. Blameless debriefs after every exercise feed action items back into the programme, ensuring the scenario library and playbooks evolve with the service.
